title: Cat Facial Drooping: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn about the causes of facial drooping in cats, why it's a medium-urgency concern, and how photos help your vet provide the best care.
content_html:
What is Facial Drooping in Cats?

Facial drooping, often referred to as facial nerve paralysis, occurs when the Seventh Cranial Nerve—which controls the muscles of facial expression—becomes damaged or inflamed. This leads to a loss of muscle tone on one side of the face. You may notice your cat has a saggy lip, an inability to blink, a drooping ear, or is dropping food while eating.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. In most cases, facial drooping is not an immediate life-threatening emergency, but it does require a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours. Prompt care is necessary because the eye on the affected side may not be able to blink, leading to painful corneal ulcers. However, if the drooping is accompanied by a head tilt, circling, or sudden collapse, you should seek emergency care immediately.

The Importance of Visual Documentation

Providing your veterinarian with a clear photo or video of your cat in their home environment is a vital triage tool. Stress during a car ride or at the clinic can cause a cat to tense up, potentially masking the degree of muscle weakness. A photo allows the vet to see the symptoms at their most natural, helping them differentiate between nerve issues, ear infections, or other underlying conditions.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of facial drooping in cats?
faqs.answer: The most frequent causes include middle or inner ear infections (otitis media/interna), inflammatory polyps, trauma to the head, or idiopathic facial paralysis where the cause is unknown.

faqs.question: Can a cat recover from facial nerve paralysis?
faqs.answer: Yes, many cats experience a full or partial recovery depending on the underlying cause. If the drooping is caused by an infection that is treated quickly, the prognosis is often very good.

faqs.question: How will my vet diagnose the issue?
faqs.answer: Your vet will likely perform a neurological exam, check deep inside the ear canal with an otoscope, and may recommend blood work or imaging like X-rays or a CT scan to look for infections or growths.
